disease.name: CMV Hepatitis
disease.note: CMV hepatitis in immunocompetent (mononucleosis-like syndrome) and immunocompromised (transplant, HIV - more severe). Congenital CMV: leading infectious cause of neonatal jaundice with sensorineural hearing loss.

severityAssessment.severityLabel: Immunocompetent mild -> OBSERVE; Immunocompromised -> URGENT treatment; Congenital -> SPECIALIST; Disseminated -> EMERGENCY

examFeatures.note: Congenital CMV: blueberry muffin rash, petechiae

subtypes.name: Acute CMV in Immunocompetent
subtypes.note: Mononucleosis-like syndrome with fever, fatigue, mild hepatitis. Usually self-limited. Less pharyngitis compared to EBV.

subtypes.name: CMV in Immunocompromised
subtypes.note: Severe disseminated disease: hepatitis, pneumonitis, colitis, retinitis, encephalitis. High morbidity and mortality if untreated.

subtypes.name: Congenital CMV
subtypes.note: Leading infectious cause of neonatal jaundice. Features: IUGR, petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly, jaundice, microcephaly, sensorineural hearing loss, chorioretinitis.

emergency.action: IV ganciclovir 5 mg/kg q12h for severe/disseminated CMV in immunocompromised
ICU supportive care for multiorgan failure

inpatient.action: IV ganciclovir or oral valganciclovir 900 mg PO BID for moderate-severe disease
Reduce immunosuppression if possible in transplant recipients
Monitor CMV PCR weekly until negative
Supportive care for immunocompetent: hydration, rest, antipyretics

outpatient.action: Valganciclovir 900 mg PO BID for mild-moderate CMV in immunocompromised (step-down)
Monitor CMV PCR q1-2 weeks until clearance
Secondary prophylaxis after treatment until immune recovery

refractory.action: Foscarnet or cidofovir for ganciclovir-resistant CMV (UL97 mutation)
CMV hyperimmune globulin in transplant settings

preventive.action: CMV prophylaxis in high-risk transplant: valganciclovir 900 mg PO daily
Preemptive therapy: monitor CMV PCR weekly, initiate treatment at predefined threshold
Valacyclovir prophylaxis in CMV D+/R- kidney transplant
Avoid CMV-positive blood products in CMV-negative transplant recipients

disease.pathophysiology: CMV infects hepatocytes, biliary epithelium, endothelial cells, and monocytes. Direct cytopathic effect causes cell lysis and microabscesses. CD8+ T-cell mediated inflammation drives granulomatous hepatitis pattern. In immunocompromised, unchecked viral replication leads to widespread tissue involvement. Congenital CMV: transplacental transmission; virus targets neural progenitor cells and inner ear causing hearing loss, and infects hepatocytes causing cholestatic jaundice.

diagnosticClues.clue: Fever + hepatitis + atypical lymphocytosis in immunocompetent (mono-like but less pharyngitis)
CMV PCR positive in blood in setting of elevated LFTs
Immunocompromised host with fever, hepatitis, and high CMV viral load
Neonatal jaundice + petechiae + microcephaly + sensorineural hearing loss
Transplant recipient with fever and transaminitis

exclusionClues.clue: Negative CMV PCR and CMV IgM
Immunocompetent CMV IgG positive only (past infection)
